name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَلَمَّا جَآءَهُمْ كِتَـٰبٌ مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ مُصَدِّقٌ لِّمَا مَعَهُمْ وَكَانُوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ يَسْتَفْتِحُونَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُم مَّا عَرَفُوا۟ كَفَرُوا۟ بِهِۦ ۚ فَلَعْنَةُ ٱللَّهِ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٨٩)
```

```english
And when there came to them a Book from Allah, confirming that which is with them—and earlier they would pray for victory over the pagans—so when there came to them what they recognized, they denied it. So may the curse of Allah be on the faithless! (89)
```

```arabic
بِئْسَمَا ٱشْتَرَوْا۟ بِهِۦٓ أَنفُسَهُمْ أَن يَكْفُرُوا۟ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ بَغْيًا أَن يُنَزِّلَ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ عَلَىٰ مَن يَشَآءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِۦ ۖ فَبَآءُو بِغَضَبٍ عَلَىٰ غَضَبٍ ۚ وَلِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (٩٠)
```

```english
Evil is that for which they have sold their souls, by defying what Allah has sent down, out of envy that Allah should bestow His grace on any of His servants that He wishes. Thus, they earned wrath upon wrath, and there is a humiliating punishment for the faithless. (90)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمْ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ قَالُوا۟ نُؤْمِنُ بِمَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا وَيَكْفُرُونَ بِمَا وَرَآءَهُۥ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا مَعَهُمْ ۗ قُلْ فَلِمَ تَقْتُلُونَ أَنۢبِيَآءَ ٱللَّهِ مِن قَبْلُ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٩١)
```

```english
And when they are told, ‘Believe in what Allah has sent down,’ they say, ‘We believe in what was sent down to us,’ and they disbelieve what is besides it, though it is the truth confirming what is with them. Say, ‘Then why would you kill the prophets of Allah formerly, should you be faithful?’ (91)
```

```arabic
۞ وَلَقَدْ جَآءَكُم مُّوسَىٰ بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ ثُمَّ ٱتَّخَذْتُمُ ٱلْعِجْلَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ وَأَنتُمْ ظَـٰلِمُونَ (٩٢)
```

```english
Certainly Moses brought you manifest proofs, but then you took up the Calf in his absence and you were wrongdoers. (92)
```

```arabic
وَإِذْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَكُمْ وَرَفَعْنَا فَوْقَكُمُ ٱلطُّورَ خُذُوا۟ مَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰكُم بِقُوَّةٍ وَٱسْمَعُوا۟ ۖ قَالُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا وَعَصَيْنَا وَأُشْرِبُوا۟ فِى قُلُوبِهِمُ ٱلْعِجْلَ بِكُفْرِهِمْ ۚ قُلْ بِئْسَمَا يَأْمُرُكُم بِهِۦٓ إِيمَـٰنُكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٩٣)
```

```english
And when We took covenant with you and raised the Mount above you, [declaring], ‘Hold on with power to what We have given you, and do listen!’ They said, ‘We hear, and disobey,’ and their hearts had been imbued with [the love of] the Calf, due to their unfaith. Say, ‘Evil is that to which your faith prompts you, should you be faithful!’ (93)
```

## (Commentary)

**His saying, the Exalted:** ولما جائهم (And when there came to them...) etc. The context indicates that "this Book" refers to the Quran.

**And His saying:** وكانوا من قبل يستفتحون على الذين كفروا (And before that they would pray for victory over the faithless). This refers to the occurrence of harassment against them by the Arab disbelievers, and that they used to seek victory (يستفتحون)—*to pray for triumph over their enemies*—through the advent of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and his migration. This seeking of victory had continued from them before the migration (الهجرة) to such an extent that the Arab disbelievers also knew of it from them, as indicated by the use of the past continuous "they used to" (كانوا). **And His saying:** فلما جائهم ما عرفوا (So when there came to them what they recognized). That is, they recognized that he was the one, due to the correspondence of the descriptions they possessed with him, yet they disbelieved.

**His saying, the Exalted:** بئسما إشتروا (Evil is that for which they have sold...) is an explanation of the reason for their disbelief after having knowledge, and that the sole reason for this was rebellion (البغي) and envy (الحسد). Thus, His saying بغياً (out of envy) is a cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق نوعي) describing the motive. His saying أن ينزل الله (that Allah should send down) is connected to it. **And His saying, the Exalted:** فبائوا بغضب على غضب (So they drew [Allah’s] wrath upon wrath). That is, they returned accompanied by, or enveloped in, wrath due to their disbelief in the Quran, added upon the wrath due to their prior disbelief in the Torah (التوراة). The meaning is that before the advent and migration, they were supporters of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) and seekers of victory through him and the Book revealed to him. Then, when the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) came to them and the Quran was revealed to him, and they recognized that he was the one through whom they used to seek victory and whose arrival they awaited, envy was stirred within them and arrogance (الإستكبار) seized them. Consequently, they disbelieved and denied what they used to mention, just as they used to disbelieve in the Torah before; thus, this act of theirs was disbelief upon disbelief.

**His saying, the Exalted:** ويكفرون بما ورائه (And they disbelieve in what is beyond it). That is, they manifest disbelief in what is beyond it; otherwise, they are also disbelievers in that which was revealed to them, namely the Torah.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قل فلم تقتلون (Say, ‘Why then did you kill...’). The letter *fa* (ف) denotes a logical derivation (التفريع). The question is derived from their claim: "We believe in what was revealed to us." That is, if your saying "We believe in what was revealed to us" were true and sincere, then why did you kill the prophets of Allah? And why did you disbelieve in Moses by taking the calf? And why did you say, upon the taking of the covenant and the raising of the Mount: "We hear and we disobey"?

**His saying, the Exalted:** واشربوا في قلوبهم العجل (And their hearts were made to drink [the love of] the calf). "Making to drink" (الإشراب) is to give water. What is meant by "the calf" is the love of the calf; it was placed in its stead for emphasis (المبالغة), as if they had been made to drink the calf itself. His saying "in their hearts" (في قلوبهم) relates to this. Thus, the speech contains two metaphors, or a metaphor and a metonymy.

**His saying, the Exalted:** قل بئسما يأمركم به إيمانكم (Say, ‘Evil is that which your faith commands you...’). This is in the position of drawing a conclusion from the arguments presented against them regarding the killing of prophets, disbelief in Moses, and arrogance in announcing disobedience. It carries a sense of mockery (الاستهزاء) toward them.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: **وَلَمَّا جَاءَهُمْ كِتَابٌ مِّنْ عِندِ اللَّهِ مُصَدِّقٌ** (And when there came to them a Book from Allah, confirming...) the verse. He (peace be upon him) said: "The Jews used to find in their books that the place of migration of Muhammad, the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), was between *'Ayr* and *Uhud*. So they went out seeking that location. They passed by a mountain called *Haddad* and said: '*Haddad* and *Uhud* are the same.' So they dispersed around it; some settled in *Tayma*, some in *Fadak*, and some in *Khaybar*. Those in *Tayma* yearned for some of their brothers, and a Bedouin from the tribe of *Qays* passed by them. They hired him, and he said to them: 'I will take you through the area between *'Ayr* and *Uhud*.' They said to him: 'When you pass by them, inform us.' When he reached the middle of the land of Medina with them, he said: 'That is *'Ayr* and this is *Uhud*.' They dismounted from his camels and said to him: 'We have found our objective, so we have no need for your camels; go wherever you wish.' They wrote to their brothers in *Fadak* and *Khaybar*: 'We have found the place, so come to us.' They wrote back: 'We have settled in our homes and acquired wealth, and we are close to you; so when that [event] occurs, we will hasten to you.' They acquired wealth in the land of Medina. When their wealth increased, news of this reached *Tubba'*, so he raided them. They fortified themselves against him, and he besieged them, then he granted them amnesty. They came down to him, and he said to them: 'I have found your land pleasant, and I see myself staying among you.' They said: 'That is not for you; it is the place of migration of a Prophet, and it belongs to no one until that happens.' He said to them: 'Then I shall leave among you those of my family who, when that happens, will assist and support him.' So he left the two tribes you see: the *Aws* and the *Khazraj*. When they became numerous there, they would take the wealth of the Jews. The Jews would say to them: 'Indeed, when Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) is sent, we shall surely expel you from our homes and our wealth.' But when Allah sent Muhammad, the *Ansar* believed in him while the Jews disbelieved in him. This is [the meaning of] His saying, the Exalted: 'And before that they would pray for victory over the faithless...' to the end of the verse."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Ishaq, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Abu Nu'aym (in *al-Dala'il*) narrated from Ibn Abbas that the Jews used to seek victory over the *Aws* and the *Khazraj* by the Messenger of Allah before his mission. When Allah sent him from among the Arabs, they disbelieved in him and denied what they used to say about him. Mu'adh ibn Jabal, Bishr ibn al-Bara, and Dawud ibn Salama said to them: "O assembly of Jews! Be God-conscious (اتقوا الله) and submit (أسلموا), for you used to seek victory over us through Muhammad when we were people of polytheism (شرك), and you would tell us that he would be sent and describe him by his attributes." Salam ibn Mishkam, one of the Banu al-Nadir, said: "He has not brought us anything we recognize, and he is not the one we used to mention to you." So Allah revealed: **وَلَمَّا جَاءَهُمْ كِتَابٌ مِّنْ عِندِ اللَّهِ** (And when there came to them a Book from Allah...) the verse.

Also in *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abu Nu'aym narrated in *al-Dala'il* via 'Ata and al-Dahhak from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The Jews of Banu Qurayza and al-Nadir, before Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) was sent, used to seek victory from Allah, praying against those who disbelieved, saying: 'O Allah, we ask Your help by the right of the Unlettered Prophet (النبي الأمي) that You grant us victory over them,' and they would be granted victory. But when there came to them what they recognized—meaning Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم)—and they had no doubt about him, they disbelieved in him."

**I say:** Narrations close to these two meanings have been reported through other chains as well. Some commentators, after referring to the last narration and its likes, said: "Despite the weakness of its narrators and its contradiction to other reported narrations, it is anomalous (شاذة) in meaning by making the 'seeking of victory' (الاستفتاح) a supplication by the person of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), and in some [versions] 'by his right' (بحقه). This is not legislated, and no one has a right (حق) over Allah such that He should be supplicated by it." End of quote.

This [objection] arises from a lack of reflection on the meaning of "right" (الحق) and the meaning of an "oath" (القسم). To explain: An oath is the binding of a statement or an origination (الإنشاء) to something possessing nobility (شرافة) and dignity (كرامة) by virtue of its being noble or dignified, such that its nobility or dignity would be nullified by the nullification of the linguistic relation. If it is a statement (خبر), it is by the nullification of its truthfulness; if it is an origination—a command or prohibition—it is by the failure to comply with the obligation. If you say: "By my life (لعمري), Zayd is standing," you have bound the truth of your speech to the nobility of your life and existence, making it contingent upon it, such that if your statement were false, your life would be devoid of nobility. Similarly, if you say: "Do such-and-half, by my life," or "I adjure you by my life to do such-and-such," you have bound your command to the honor of your life, such that if your addressee does not comply, the honor of your life and the value of your existence would vanish.

From this, it becomes clear, firstly: that the oath is the highest level of emphasis (التأكيد) in speech, as mentioned by the people of literature.

**Secondly:** The object of the oath (المقسم به) must be more noble than that which it qualifies; there is no sense in emphasizing speech with something lower than it in honor and dignity. Allah, the Exalted, has sworn in His Book by His own Name and Attribute, such as His saying: **وَاللَّهِ رَبِّنَا** (By Allah, our Lord) and His saying: **فَوَرَبِّكَ لَنَسْأَلَنَّهُمْ** (By your Lord, We will surely question them) and His saying: **فَبِعِزَّتِكَ لَأُغْوِيَنَّهُمْ** (By Your might, I will surely lead them all astray). He also swore by His Prophet, His angels, and His Books, and He swore by His creations such as the heaven, the earth, the sun, the moon, the stars, the night, the day, the mountains, the seas, the lands, man, trees, the fig, and the olive. This is only because they possess a true nobility (شرافة حقة) by Allah’s ennobling them, and a dignity (كرامة) before Allah, insofar as each of them is either a possessor of one of His holy and dignified attributes by the dignity of His transcendent Essence, or an act attributed to the Source of Splendor and Sanctity—and everything is noble by the nobility of His noble Essence. So what prevents a supplicant among us, when asking Allah for something, from asking Him by one of these things, insofar as Allah, the Glorified, has ennobled it and sworn by it? And what has made the matter so trivial specifically regarding the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) such that he is excluded from this universal principle and excepted from this general rule?

By my life (ولعمري), the Messenger of Allah, Muhammad (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم), is not more trivial before Allah than an Iraqi fig or a Syrian olive, and Allah has sworn by his noble person, saying: **لَعَمْرُكَ إِنَّهُمْ لَفِي سَكْرَتِهِمْ يَعْمَهُونَ** (By your life, they were surely bewildered in their intoxication) [Al-Hijr: 72].

Furthermore, "the right" (الحق)—the opposite of which is "the false" (الباطل)—is that which is established and real (الثابت الواقع) in external reality insofar as it is so, such as the earth, man, and every matter established in itself. This includes financial rights and other social rights, as they are established from the perspective of society. The Quran has nullified everything claimed as a right except what Allah has realized and established, whether in creation (الإيجاد) or in legislation (التشريع). Thus, the right in the world of legislation and the domain of religious society is what Allah has made a right, such as financial rights, the rights of brothers, and the rights of parents over the child. He, the Glorified, is not subject to the judgment of anyone such that they could impose upon Him what He must fulfill, as might appear from some Mu'tazilite arguments. However, it is possible for Him to impose a right upon Himself—an imposition according to the language of legislation—such that it becomes a right for another upon Him, the Exalted, as He said: **كَذَٰلِكَ حَقًّا عَلَيْنَا نُنجِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** (Even so, it is a duty incumbent upon Us to deliver the faithful) [Yunus: 103], and He said: **وَلَقَدْ سَبَقَتْ كَلِمَتُنَا لِعِبَادِنَا الْمُرْسَلِينَ . إِنَّهُمْ لَهُمُ الْمَنصُورُونَ . وَإِنَّ جُندَنَا لَهُمُ الْغَالِبُونَ** (Certainly Our decree has gone forth for Our servants, the apostles: that they will indeed be the ones to be helped, and that Our host will indeed be the victors) [As-Saffat: 171-173].

Victory (النصر), as you see, is absolute, not restricted by anything. Thus, deliverance is a right for the believers upon Allah, and victory is a right for the Messenger upon Allah, the Exalted. Allah has ennobled this right by making it His own; thus, it was an act from Him, attributed to Him, and ennobled by Him. Therefore, there is no barrier to swearing by it to Allah, the Exalted, for He is the One who establishes and ennobles the right and swears by every noble matter.

Once you understand what we have mentioned, you will know that there is no barrier to Allah swearing by His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله‌وسلم) or by the right of His Prophet, and likewise His swearing by His pure friends (أوليائه) or by their right, for He has established for them a right upon Himself to grant them victory on the path of felicity (صراط السعادة) with every victory related to it, as you have learned.

As for the saying of the objector: "No one has a right upon Allah," it is a weak statement.

Yes, there is no right upon Allah that another establishes against Him, such that He would be subject to the judgment of another or overcome by the power of someone else. No one disputes this. Nor does the supplicant call upon Him by a right that another has imposed upon Him, but rather by what He, the Exalted, has established for Himself through His promise that is never broken. This is the matter.
